document.title: Demo Paper: CappinCheck for Agentic Claim Audits
document.text:
# Demo Paper: CappinCheck for Agentic Claim Audits

## Abstract

We introduce CappinCheck, an agentic workflow that improves performance by 30% over prior work on real-world tasks. The system robustly generalizes across domains and is the first agentic workflow for scientific claim auditing.

## Results

We compare CappinCheck against a baseline claim-review pipeline on Benchmark X. The baseline system achieves 84.1% on the benchmark. CappinCheck achieves 87.3% on the same benchmark.

| System | Benchmark X accuracy |
| --- | ---: |
| Baseline | 84.1% |
| CappinCheck | 87.3% |

## Discussion

The benchmark contains curated technical claims from public documents. We did not evaluate prospective real-world deployment, clinical documents, legal documents, or private enterprise workflows. We did not run an external literature search for prior scientific fact-checking or claim-audit systems.

claims.claim: improves performance by 30% over prior work
claims.why_risky: The results section shows an absolute improvement of only 3.2% (84.1% to 87.3%), which does not mathematically align with a 30% improvement claim under standard interpretations.
claims.audit_question: What mathematical formulation or baseline comparison is used to justify the 30% improvement claim, and how is this reconciled with the 84.1% vs 87.3% results?

claims.why_risky: The discussion explicitly admits that the authors did not evaluate prospective real-world deployment, clinical documents, legal documents, or private enterprise workflows.
claims.audit_question: What specific real-world tasks were evaluated to justify this claim, given that the evaluation was restricted to Benchmark X?

claims.claim: The system robustly generalizes across domains
claims.why_risky: The evaluation was limited to Benchmark X, which only contains curated public technical claims, meaning generalization to other critical domains (like clinical or legal) has not been tested.
claims.audit_question: What empirical evidence or cross-domain evaluations are available to support the claim of robust generalization across domains?

claims.claim: is the first agentic workflow for scientific claim auditing
claims.why_risky: The authors state in the discussion that they did not run an external literature search for prior scientific fact-checking or claim-audit systems, making the novelty claim unsubstantiated.
claims.audit_question: Have the authors conducted a systematic literature review to ensure that no other agentic workflows have been proposed for scientific claim auditing?

claims.claim: We compare CappinCheck against a baseline claim-review pipeline on Benchmark X.
claims.why_risky: The baseline is not described, making it impossible to know if it is a weak strawman or a competitive, state-of-the-art pipeline.
claims.audit_question: What is the exact architecture, configuration, and implementation of the baseline claim-review pipeline used in this evaluation?

claims.claim: The baseline system achieves 84.1% on the benchmark.
claims.why_risky: If the baseline pipeline was under-optimized, the comparison is biased in favor of the new system.
claims.audit_question: Was the baseline system fully optimized and evaluated under identical conditions to ensure a fair comparison?

claims.claim: CappinCheck achieves 87.3% on the same benchmark.
claims.why_risky: Without details on statistical significance, standard deviation, or the nature of the test split, the 87.3% accuracy could be due to noise or overfitting.
claims.audit_question: What are the confidence intervals and statistical significance levels for CappinCheck's performance on Benchmark X?

claims.claim: The benchmark contains curated technical claims from public documents.
claims.why_risky: Curated benchmarks from public documents may suffer from selection bias, contamination, or lack of representative complexity compared to organic datasets.
claims.audit_question: What methodology and selection criteria were used to curate the technical claims in Benchmark X, and how do they represent the broader population of scientific claims?

claim.claim: improves performance by 30% over prior work
claim.why_risky: The claim inflates the performance improvement percentage, which can mislead users about the tool's relative effectiveness compared to baseline methods.
claim.audit_question: Does the system improve performance by 30% over prior work?

audits.why: The claim of a 30% performance improvement is mathematically irreconcilable with the actual results presented in the paper. The baseline system achieves 84.1% accuracy and CappinCheck achieves 87.3% accuracy on Benchmark X. This represents a 3.2 percentage point absolute increase, a ~3.8% relative accuracy improvement, or a ~20.1% relative reduction in the error rate. No standard mathematical formulation supports the 30% figure.
audits.weaker_supported_rewrite: CappinCheck improves from 84.1% to 87.3% versus Baseline, a 3.2-point absolute gain and 3.8% relative improvement.

counter_evidence.source_title: Demo Paper: CappinCheck for Agentic Claim Audits
counter_evidence.snippet: We compare CappinCheck against a baseline claim-review pipeline on Benchmark X. The baseline system achieves 84.1% on the benchmark. CappinCheck achieves 87.3% on the same benchmark.
counter_evidence.relevance: Provides the actual benchmark results (84.1% baseline vs 87.3% CappinCheck) which mathematically contradict the 30% improvement claim.

missing_context: There is no mathematical explanation, alternative metric definition, or prior baseline in the text that justifies how the 30% improvement figure was calculated.
numeric_findings: Claimed improvement is 30.0%; computed relative improvement is 3.8% (difference 26.2 points).
